input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

data.content: Good — both .NET 9 and .NET 10 SDKs/runtimes are installed. I'll create a BenchmarkDotNet project targeting both frameworks.
data.reasoningText:
Both .NET 9 and .NET 10 runtimes are available. I need to create a BenchmarkDotNet project that benchmarks the three methods (`AverageWordLength`, `WordLengthHistogram`, `UniqueWordCount`) and targets both `net9.0` and `net10.0` so BenchmarkDotNet can compare them side by side.

Let me create a benchmark project.

toolCalls.name: create

toolResult.sessionLog:
diff --git a/tmp/sv-96df3fd84ffb4bbaa5ed764459c79230/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj b/tmp/sv-96df3fd84ffb4bbaa5ed764459c79230/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj
create file mode 100644
index 0000000..0000000
--- a/dev/null
+++ b/tmp/sv-96df3fd84ffb4bbaa5ed764459c79230/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj
@@ -1,0 +1,15 @@
+<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
+  <PropertyGroup>
+    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
+    <TargetFrameworks>net9.0;net10.0</TargetFrameworks>
+    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
+    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
+  </PropertyGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <PackageReference Include="BenchmarkDotNet" Version="0.14.*" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <Compile Include="../TextAnalyzer/TextAnalyzer.cs" Link="TextAnalyzer.cs" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+</Project>
+

data.reasoningText:
The `RuntimeMoniker.Net100` doesn't exist yet in BenchmarkDotNet 0.14. Let me check what monikers are available. I need to use a different approach - maybe `Net10_0` or just use `NativeAot100` or something. Let me check the BenchmarkDotNet source or use a different approach.

Actually, for .NET 10, BenchmarkDotNet might not have a moniker yet since it's very new. Let me use a different approach - I'll use custom jobs with a runtime specification, or just use `[SimpleJob]` without runtime monikers and let the multi-targeting handle it.
 
I realize the simplest solution is to skip runtime monikers entirely and let BenchmarkDotNet run on whatever framework the project targets. To actually compare .NET 9 and .NET 10 performance though, I need to explicitly specify both runtimes either via command-line arguments or through the job configuration.

I'm checking what the correct moniker format is for .NET 10 — it's likely `Net10_0`.

arguments.command: cd /tmp/sv-96df3fd84ffb4bbaa5ed764459c79230/Benchmarks && dotnet restore 2>&1 | tail -3 && grep -r "Net10\|Net100\|Net10_0" ~/.nuget/packages/benchmarkdotnet/*/lib/net8.0/BenchmarkDotNet.xml 2>/dev/null | head -5 || echo "Checking enum values..." && dotnet run -c Release -f net10.0 -- --list flat 2>&1 | head -5

arguments.command:
python3 -c "
import zipfile, re
zf = zipfile.ZipFile('/home/runner/.nuget/packages/benchmarkdotnet/0.15.8/lib/net8.0/BenchmarkDotNet.dll')
# Actually let's just search the XML for RuntimeMoniker enum members
import subprocess
result = subprocess.run(['grep', '-i', 'runtimemoniker', '/home/runner/.nuget/packages/benchmarkdotnet/0.15.8/lib/net8.0/BenchmarkDotNet.xml'], capture_output=True, text=True)
for line in result.stdout.split('\n'):
    if 'Net' in line and ('10' in line or '9' in line):
        print(line.strip())
"
